teaching.title: Java Arrays
teaching.analogy: A Java array is a row of numbered lockers — fixed count, each slot holds one specific type. You declare the count upfront and it never changes. Every locker is zero-indexed.
teaching.content: Java arrays: fixed size declared at creation. Syntax: int[] arr = new int[5]; or int[] arr = {1,2,3}. Zero-indexed. Access: arr[i]. Length: arr.length (no parentheses). Out-of-bounds throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.

examples.title: Declaring & Using Arrays
2D Arrays
examples.explanation: Array of arrays — rows and columns.

teaching.funFact: Java arrays are objects on the heap! That's why .length is a field (not a method like .length()). Arrays inherit from Object — you can call toString(), equals(), etc. on them! 🤯

teaching.title: ArrayList — Dynamic Arrays
teaching.analogy: ArrayList is a smart filing cabinet — it grows automatically when you add more folders. No need to decide the size upfront. The drawback: slightly slower than primitive arrays due to boxing overhead.
teaching.content: ArrayList<T> is a resizable array from java.util. Add: add(item). Get: get(i). Size: size(). Remove: remove(i) or remove(Object). Contains: contains(x). Requires import java.util.ArrayList.

examples.title: ArrayList Operations
examples.explanation: Dynamic sizing — no fixed count needed.

teaching.funFact: ArrayList uses a plain array internally and doubles its size when full. Inserting at the END is O(1) amortized. Inserting in the MIDDLE copies all subsequent elements — O(n). LinkedList is better for mid-list insertion! ⚡

mcq.q: int[] arr = new int[4]. What is arr.length?
mcq.explanation: arr.length: The .length field (not method!) of an array equals the number of elements. new int[4] creates 4 slots → length = 4.
Zero-indexed: s[0]='A', s[1]='B', s[2]='C'. Index 2 = third element = 'C'.

mcq.q: ArrayList vs array: which can grow dynamically?
mcq.explanation: ArrayList: Arrays have FIXED size set at creation. ArrayList grows automatically. ArrayList uses an array internally and resizes when needed.

mcq.q: ArrayList<String> list. How do you add 'Hello'?
mcq.explanation: .add(): ArrayList uses methods. add(item) appends to end. add(index,item) inserts at position. Arrays use arr[i]=value for assignment.

mcq.q: What exception does arr[10] throw on a 5-element array?
mcq.explanation: ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Java throws this specific exception when accessing an array with an invalid index (< 0 or >= length).

coding.title: Array length field
coding.objective: int[] a = {5,10,15,20}. What does a.length return?
coding.hint: 4 elements → length = 4. It's a field, not a method (no parentheses).

coding.title: ArrayList size
coding.objective: After add(1),add(2),add(3) on a new ArrayList. What is size()?

options: <= causes ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException at i=3. Use i < arr.length
debugging.answer: <= causes ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException at i=3. Use i < arr.length
debugging.hint: arr.length=3. arr[3] doesn't exist. Always use i < arr.length.

options: ArrayList needs wrapper types — use ArrayList<Integer> not ArrayList<int>
debugging.answer: ArrayList needs wrapper types — use ArrayList<Integer> not ArrayList<int>
debugging.hint: Java generics don't accept primitives. Use Integer, Double, Boolean (wrapper classes) not int, double, boolean.

teaching.title: Java String Methods
teaching.analogy: Java String is an immutable object with a built-in toolbox of methods. Every 'modification' creates a NEW String — the original stays unchanged. Like a stone tablet you can copy and modify the copy but never erase the original.
teaching.content: Java String: immutable object. Key methods: length(), charAt(i), substring(i,j), indexOf(s), contains(s), startsWith(s), endsWith(s), toUpperCase(), toLowerCase(), trim(), replace(old,new), split(regex).

examples.title: Essential String Methods
examples.explanation: String methods return new Strings — never modify the original.
examples.code:
String s = "Hello, World!";
System.out.println(s.length());       // 13
System.out.println(s.charAt(0));      // H
System.out.println(s.substring(7));   // World!
System.out.println(s.substring(0,5)); // Hello
System.out.println(s.indexOf("World")); // 7
System.out.println(s.toUpperCase());  // HELLO, WORLD!
System.out.println(s.replace('l','L')); // HeLLo, WorLd!
System.out.println(s.contains("World")); // true

String[] parts = "a,b,c".split(",");
// parts = ["a","b","c"]

teaching.funFact: Java String interning: identical String literals share the same object in memory (the String Pool). That's why 'hello' == 'hello' is often true for literals but false for new String('hello') — they're different objects! 🏊

teaching.title: StringBuilder — Mutable Strings
teaching.analogy: StringBuilder is a whiteboard — you can erase, append, insert, and delete without creating new objects. Use it when you need to build a String piece-by-piece in a loop. String concatenation in a loop is VERY slow — StringBuilder is the fix.
teaching.content: StringBuilder is mutable. append(s), insert(i,s), delete(i,j), reverse(), charAt(i), length(), toString(). Dramatically faster than String + String in loops — O(n) vs O(n²) for concatenation.

examples.title: StringBuilder vs String concat

teaching.funFact: String + String in a loop is O(n²) — Java creates a new String for each concatenation, copying all previous characters. StringBuilder is O(n) because it maintains a resizable buffer internally. This matters at scale! ⚡

mcq.explanation: length(): 'Java' has 4 characters → length() = 4. Note: String uses length() WITH parentheses (it's a method). Arrays use .length WITHOUT parentheses (it's a field).
substring(1,3): start=1 included, end=3 excluded. Characters at index 1='e', 2='l'. Result: 'el'.

mcq.q: Why use StringBuilder instead of String + in a loop?
options: String + creates a new object each iteration — O(n²). StringBuilder is O(n)
mcq.explanation: Performance: Each String + creates a new immutable String object copying all previous characters. For 1000 concatenations, that's ~500,000 character copies! StringBuilder maintains a buffer.

options: No — Strings are immutable, returns a new String
mcq.explanation: Immutable: Strings in Java cannot be modified. toUpperCase() returns a NEW String. The original 'hello' is unchanged. Always assign the result: s = s.toUpperCase();

options: String is immutable — must assign the result: s = s.toUpperCase();
debugging.answer: String is immutable — must assign the result: s = s.toUpperCase();
debugging.hint: toUpperCase() returns a NEW String but doesn't modify s. Always reassign: s = s.toUpperCase();

prediction.explanation: Start: 'Code'. append: 'CodeClash'. reverse: 'hsalCedoC'. Last charAccess reversed.

teaching.title: HashMap — Key-Value Store
teaching.analogy: HashMap is a dictionary — you look up words (keys) to find definitions (values). Given any key, retrieval is O(1) — instant. Unlike arrays where you search by index, HashMap searches by meaningful name.
teaching.content: HashMap<K,V> stores key-value pairs. put(k,v), get(k), containsKey(k), remove(k), size(), keySet(), values(), entrySet(). Keys must be unique. Allows one null key. Does NOT maintain insertion order (use LinkedHashMap for order).

examples.title: HashMap Operations

teaching.funFact: HashMap is implemented as an array of 'buckets' where each bucket holds a linked list of entries. Java 8 upgraded: when a bucket gets ≥8 entries, it converts to a Red-Black Tree for O(log n) lookup! 🌳

mcq.q: HashMap<String,Int> map. How do you store key='x', value=5?
mcq.explanation: .put(key,value): The method to store in a HashMap. .get(key) retrieves. Unlike arrays which use [], HashMap uses methods.

mcq.q: HashMap.get() for a missing key returns:
mcq.explanation: null: HashMap.get() returns null if key doesn't exist. Use containsKey() or getOrDefault(key, fallback) to handle missing keys safely.

options: No — use LinkedHashMap for insertion order
mcq.explanation: No Order: HashMap makes no guarantees about iteration order. Use LinkedHashMap to preserve insertion order, TreeMap for sorted order.

mcq.q: Duplicate key in HashMap.put() — what happens?
options: Value is updated to the new value
mcq.explanation: Overwrite: put() with an existing key REPLACES the old value. No exception. The old value is returned by put(). Keys are always unique in HashMap.

coding.title: HashMap get
Missing key
coding.objective: HashMap with only key 'x'. What does get('y') return?

options: m.get('points') returns null — null > 50 throws NullPointerException. Check containsKey first.
debugging.answer: m.get('points') returns null — null > 50 throws NullPointerException. Check containsKey first.
debugging.hint: Always check containsKey() before get() to avoid NullPointerException on missing keys.

prediction.explanation: put('a',99) overwrites the first put('a',1). Only 2 unique keys: 'a','b'. get('a')=99, size()=2.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE COLLECTION MASTER
teaching.analogy: The Collection Master wields arrays, ArrayLists, Strings, and HashMaps simultaneously. It knows every method, every gotcha, every O(n) vs O(1) trade-off. Prove you've mastered Java's data layer!
teaching.content: Tests ALL Level 4 Java: array syntax/length/OOB, ArrayList add/get/size/remove, String methods (immutability, length(), substring(), indexOf()), StringBuilder append/reverse, HashMap put/get/null keys.

examples.title: Full Java Data Arsenal
examples.code:
✅ int[] arr = new int[n] or {1,2,3}
✅ arr.length (field, not method!)
✅ ArrayList<Integer> — dynamic, add/get/size
✅ String immutable — always reassign!
✅ substring(i,j) — end excluded
✅ StringBuilder — use in loops
✅ HashMap<K,V> — put/get/containsKey
✅ get() missing key → null!

teaching.funFact: Java's Collections Framework (ArrayList, HashMap, etc.) was introduced in Java 1.2 (1998) and is one of the most copied APIs in software history. Python's list and dict borrow many design ideas from Java Collections! 🌍

mcq.q: int[] vs ArrayList: which has .length (no parens)?
mcq.explanation: Array uses .length (field, no ()). ArrayList uses .size() (method, with ()). This is a common Java confusion point!
Immutable: toUpperCase() returns a NEW String but doesn't modify s. s is still 'hi'. Must assign: s = s.toUpperCase().

mcq.q: HashMap: put same key twice with different values — result?
mcq.explanation: Overwrite: put() with existing key replaces the old value. Keys must be unique. Previous value is returned (or null if was absent).
No primitives in generics: Java generics only accept reference types. Use Integer, Double, Boolean (wrapper classes) not int, double, boolean.

coding.title: Boss: Array + for-each
coding.objective: int[] a={2,4,6}; sum with for-each. What is sum?

options: IndexOutOfBoundsException — list has indices 0,1 only. list.get(2) doesn't exist.
debugging.answer: IndexOutOfBoundsException — list has indices 0,1 only. list.get(2) doesn't exist.
debugging.hint: Two elements → indices 0,1. get(2) is out of bounds.
